highway_name: Governor Ritchie Highway
source_note: Named in honor of Albert C. Ritchie, 49th Governor of Maryland for a record four terms.

highway_name: Aris T. Allen Boulevard
source_note: Named in honor of Dr. Aris T. Allen, who was the first African-American chair of the Maryland Republican party and the first African-American to run for a statewide office in Maryland.

highway_name: Paul T. Pitcher Memorial Highway
source_note: Named in honor of Paul T. Pitcher, an Anne Arundel County Executive who originally conceived MD Route 100.

highway_name: This Highway Dedicated to Senator John A. Cade
source_note: Dedicated to John A. Cade, former Maryland Senator who was instrumental in securing funding for I-97.

highway_name: Generals Highway
source_note: Named in honor of General George Washington who traveled this route in December 1783 as part of his journey from New York to Annapolis to resign as Commander-in-Chief.

highway_name: This Highway Dedicated to Governor Theodore R. McKeldin
source_note: Dedicated to Theodore Roosevelt McKeldin, 53rd Governor of Maryland. Construction of the "Baltimore Beltway" began under his watch in 1954.

highway_name: John F. Kennedy Memorial Highway
source_note: Named in honor of President John F. Kennedy, who officiated the ceremony dedicating I-95 and was assassinated 8 days later.

highway_name: Dedicated to Veterans of Foreign Wars
source_note: Dedicated to Veterans of Foreign Wars, a nonprofit veterans service organization devoted to veterans  service, legislative advocacy, and military and community service programs.

highway_name: Maryland Purple Heart Trail I-95
source_note: Let it be known that those who wear the Purple Heart have given their blood in the defense of their homeland and shall be forever revered by their countrymen.

highway_name: 175th Infantry Memorial Highway Dedicated to MD's ""Dandy 5th""
source_note: Dedicated to the 175th Infantry Regiment, one of the oldest in the U.S. formed during the American Revolution and has a garrison in Dundalk. The “Dandy 5th” has won 24 battle honors spanning every major American war.

highway_name: Vietnam Veterans Highway
source_note: Dedicated to Veterans of the Vietnam War (1959-1975). One of several dedications in Maryland honoring those who served.

highway_name: The Louis L. Goldstein Memorial Highway
source_note: Dedicated to Louis L. “Louie” Goldstein, Calvert County native and MD State Comptroller for a record 40 years, 1958-1998.

highway_name: Francis Scott Key Highway
source_note: Named in honor of Francis Scott Key, Maryland native and author of our national anthem, "The Star-Spangled Banner".

highway_name: Blue Star Memorial Highway
source_note: Marked highways that pay tribute to those who have served in the U.S. Armed Forces. The blue star was used on service flags to denote a service member fighting in the war.

highway_name: Joseph Biggs Memorial Highway
source_note: Named in honor of Joseph B. Biggs, affectionately known as “Joeby," a prominent Cecil County Commissioner in the 1970's.

highway_name: Jacob Tome Memorial Highway
source_note: Named in honor of Jacob Tome a philanthropist and founder of Tome School and Cecil County's first millionaire.

highway_name: This Highway Dedicated to Harriet Tubman
source_note: Dedicated to Harriett Tubman, Dorchester County native, the abolitionist and humanitarian who rescued over 70 enslaved families using the Underground Railroad.

highway_name: Dwight D. Eisenhower Memorial Highway
source_note: Named in honor of President Dwight D. Eisenhower who is considered ""Father of Interstate System.”

highway_name: 115th Infantry Regiment Memorial Highway
source_note: Named to honor the 115th Infantry Regiment, whose roots trace back to the American Revolutionary War, formed to protect the frontier of western Maryland.

highway_name: Vietnam Veterans Memorial Highway
source_note: Dedicated to Veterans of the Vietnam War (1959-1975). One of several dedications in Maryland honoring those who served.

highway_name: 29th Infantry Division Memorial Highway
source_note: Dedicated to 29th Infantry Division of the U.S. Army, formed in 1917, this decorated unit is most famous for its participation in the D-Day invasion of Normandy during World War II.

highway_name: Dedicated to Senator Robert H. Kittleman
source_note: Dedicated to former Senator Robert H. Kittleman, who served in the House of Delegates for 19 years and lobbied for the extension of MD 32 to include the River Hill community.

highway_name: Augustine Herman Highway
source_note: Named in honor of Augustine Herman, 17th century explorer, merchant and cartographer and the First Lord of Bohemia Manor, the estate that he founded in present day southeastern Cecil County.

highway_name: Baltimore-Washington Parkway Dedicated to Gladys Noon Spellman
source_note: Dedicated to Gladys Noon Spellman, educator, politician and visionary who spearheaded major social and political reform towards women’s rights and to prevent discrimination against the handicapped and elderly.

highway_name: Robert Crain Highway
source_note: Named in honor of Robert S. Crain, Charles County native and attorney who spear-headed the efforts to build a highway that connected the tobacco fields of southern Maryland with Baltimore City.

highway_name: Martin Luther King Jr. Highway
source_note: Named in honor of Martin Luther King, Jr., Nobel Peace Prize recipient and a leader in the African-American Civil Rights Movement.

highway_name: Maryland Veterans Memorial Highway
source_note: Dedicated to those from Maryland who served in the armed forces.

highway_name: Korean War & Korea Service Veterans Memorial Highway
source_note: Dedicated to Veterans of the Korean Wars, the signs were erected to help erase the stigma of “The Forgotten War”.

highway_name: Dedicated to Delegate Richard C. Matthews
source_note: Dedicated to Richard C. Matthews, native Hampstead resident and prominent business owner who represented Carroll County in the Maryland House of Delegates from 1967-1994.

highway_name: Patriot Parkway
source_note: Named in honor of victims and heroes lost during the tragic events of September 11, 2001 and subsequent military actions at home and abroad.

highway_name: John Hanson Highway
source_note: Named in honor of John Hanson, Jr., native Marylander and Patriot who recruited and equipped members of George Washington’s Revolutionary armies.

highway_name: Heroes Highway Senior Deputy Patrick Dailey Deputy First Class Mark Logsdon
source_note: Dedicated to Harford County Sheriff's Office Senior Deputy Patrick B. Dailey and Deputy First Class Mark F. Logsdon, who were killed in the line of duty in Abingdon on February 10, 2016.

highway_name: Dedicated to Delegate Michael H. Weir, Sr.
source_note: Dedicated to Michael H. Weir, Sr. who served 28 years in the Maryland General Assembly and was a member of the Chesapeake Bay Commission.

highway_name: Dedicated as Cal Ripken Way
source_note: Named in honor of Cal Ripken, Jr., native Marylander and National Baseball Hame of Fame inductee in 2007, who played his entire 21-year major league career for the Baltimore Orioles.

highway_name: Broening Highway
source_note: Named in honor of William Frederick Broening, former Mayor of Baltimore for two terms, 1919-1923 and 1927-1931.

highway_name: Dedicated to DFC Jason C. Schwenz and Ofc Michael S. Nickerson
source_note: Dedicated to Queen Anne’s County Sheriff’s Department Deputy Jason Charles Schwenz and Centreville Police Officer Michael Scott Nickerson who were killed in the line of duty responding to a noise complaint on February 13, 2001.

highway_name: Dedicated to Lt. Michael Howe
source_note: Dedicated to Baltimore County Police Lieutenant Michael Patrick Howe who passed in the line of duty on August 11, 2008.

highway_name: Dedicated to Police Officer Jason L. Schneider
source_note: Dedicated to Baltimore County Police Officer Jason L. Schneider, who was killed in the line of duty while serving an arrest warrant on August 28, 2013.

highway_name: Dedicated to Trooper Gregg A. Presbury Sr. Maryland State Police
source_note: Dedicated to Maryland State Police Trooper Gregg Alexander Presbury, Sr. who was killed in the line of duty during a traffic stop on December 17, 1977.

highway_name: Martin Boulevard
source_note: Named in honor of Glenn L. Martin, American aviation pioneer who founded the Glenn L. Martin Company which after several mergers is known today as Lockheed Martin.

highway_name: Pulaski Highway
source_note: Named in honor of Polish military commander and American Revolutionary War hero Casimir Pulaski.

highway_name: Dedicated to TFC Shaft S. Hunter Maryland State Police
source_note: Dedicated to Maryland State Police Trooper First Class Shaft Sidney Hunter who was killed in the line of duty in a fatal automobile accident on May 21, 2011.

highway_name: Dedicated to TFC Wesley W. J. Brown Maryland State Police
source_note: Dedicated to Maryland State Police Trooper First Class Wesley Washington John Brown who was shot and killed while working an off-duty security detail on June 11, 2010.

highway_name: This Highway Dedicated to Frederick Douglass
source_note: Dedicated to Frederick Douglass, Talbot County native, orator, writer and statesman who escaped slavery and became a national leader of the abolitionist movement.

highway_name: This Highway Dedicated to Senator Charles ""Mac"" Mathias
source_note: Dedicated to Charles McCurdy "Mac" Mathias, Jr., Frederick native, who represented Maryland in the United States House of Representatives (1961-1969) and United States Senate from 1969 to 1987.

highway_name: Dedicated as Korean Way
source_note: Dedicated as Korean Way as a tribute to the considerable amount of Korean-owned businesses along Route 40 and the large and ever-growing Korean-American population in Howard County.

highway_name: Korean War Veterans Memorial Highway
source_note: Dedicated to Veterans of the Korean Wars, the signs were erected to help erase the stigma of “The Forgotten War”.

highway_name: Uhl Highway
source_note: Named in honor of G. Clinton Uhl, Mt. Savage native and chairman of Maryland State Roads Commission from 1929 until his death in 1934.

highway_name: Dedicated to Deputy Lawrence H. McParlin Charles County Sheriff's Office
source_note: Dedicated to Charles County Sheriff's Office Deputy Lawrence H. McParlin who was killed in the line of duty serving a court summons on May 21, 1918; believed to be the first officer from the Charles County Sheriff's Office to perish in the line of duty.

highway_name: Dedicated to Ofc. Luke T. Hoffman Montgomery County Police Dept
source_note: Dedicated to Montgomery County Police Officer Luke T. Hoffman who was killed in the line of duty in pursuit of a suspected drunk driver on April 25, 2007.

highway_name: Dedicated to Henry Highland Garnet
source_note: Dedicated to Henry Highland Garnet, Kent County native, who was an abolitionist, minister, educator and orator and escaped slavery as a child. His "Call to Rebellion" speech in 1843 encouraged slaves to free themselves by rising up against owners.

highway_name: This Parkway Dedicated to J. Glenn Beall Jr. United States Senator
source_note: Dedicated to John Glenn Beall, Jr. Cumberland native and longtime Maryland politician, who was instrumental in securing funding for Interstate 68 and Canal Place tourism development.

highway_name: Dedicated to USMC Master Sergeant William ""Bull"" Evans
source_note: Dedicated to United States Marine Corps Master Sergeant William "Bull" Evans, Cresaptown native and decorated World War II and Korean War veteran, whose heroic acts made him a legend among Marines.

highway_name: Ali Ghan Road
source_note: Named in honor of the Ali Ghan Temple, a Shrine that opened in 1938, serving a collateral body of Freemasonry.

highway_name: Dedicated to CPL Jamel Leon Clagett Charles County Sheriff's Office
source_note: Dedicated to Charles County Sheriff’s Office Corporal Jamel Leon Clagett who was killed while driving home after work in a single vehicle crash on December 21, 2014.

highway_name: Bridge Dedicated to SSG Christopher W Swanson United States Army
source_note: Dedicated to United States Army Staff Sergeant Christopher W. Swanson, Arundel native, who was killed in action on July 22, 2006 in Al Ramadi, Iraq while supporting Operation Iraqi Freedom.

highway_name: Brooks Robinson Drive
source_note: Named in honor of Brooks Calbert Robinson, Jr., National Baseball Hame of Fame inductee in 1983, who played his entire 23-year major league  career for the Baltimore Orioles.

highway_name: This Highway Dedicated to Gold Star Mothers
source_note: Dedicated to the ongoing efforts of the Gold Star Mothers, a national group founded in 1928 dedicated to providing support and resources to mothers who have lost children during military service.

highway_name: Dedicated to Cpl Glenn Hilliard Wicomico Co Sheriffs Office
source_note: Cpl. Glenn Hilliard, killed in the line of duty June 12, 2022, served the Wicomico Co Sheriff’s Office, the Berlin Police Dept, the Crisfield Police Dept & the Ocean City Police Dept during his career. He started in law enforcement as Student Security Personnel while enrolled at the University of Maryland Eastern Shore, a historically Black university in Princess Anne.

highway_name: Hall Highway
source_note: Named in honor of Dr. Willard F. Hall of Edward W. McCready Hospital; the bridge crossing the Little Annemessex River was dedicated to him on December 17, 1922 and has been subsequently replaced in 1966.

highway_name: This Highway Dedicated to John S. Arnick
source_note: Dedicated to John S. Arnick, Baltimore native and US Marine Corps veteran, who served three separate spans in the MD House of Delegates ranging from 1969 through 2006.

highway_name: Dedicated to Senator James Clark, Jr.
source_note: Dedicated to James Clark, Jr., Ellicott City native and former president of the MD State Senate from 1979-1983.

highway_name: Clara Barton Parkway
source_note: Named in honor of Clara Barton, founder of the American Red Cross, who spent her final 15 years at her home in nearby Glen Echo which doubled as an early headquarters of the organization.

highway_name: This Parkway Dedicated In Memory of John P. Tawes
source_note: Dedicated to John P. Tawes, who advocated for the construction of a dual carriageway through the main section of Crisfield.

highway_name: This Highway Dedicated to Senator Ida G. Ruben
source_note: Dedicated to Ida G. Ruben who represented District 20 in both the MD House of Delegates and MD Senate from 1975 to 2007 and advocated for the expansion of the FDA campus in White Oak.

highway_name: Dedicated to Police Officer III Noah A Leotta | Don't Drink And Drive
source_note: Dedicated to Montgomery County Police Officer Noah A. Leotta who was killed in the line of duty while pulling over a suspected drunk driver on December 3, 2015. As a result, Senate Bill 945 was passed, colloquially referred to as “Noah’s Law."

highway_name: George Washington Highway
source_note: Named in honor of George Washington, the 1st President of the United States.

highway_name: Dedicated to Cpl. Scott A. Wheeler Howard County Police Dept
source_note: Dedicated to Howard County Police Corporal Scott A. Wheeler who was killed in the line of duty while initiating a traffic stop on June 18, 2007.

highway_name: Dedicated to Senator Philip C. Jimeno
source_note: Dedicated to Philip C. Jimeno who represented District 31 for 28 years, serving terms in both the MD House of Delegates and Senate from 1979 to 2007.

highway_name: Harriet Elizabeth Brown Memorial Parkway
source_note: Named in honor of Harriett Elizabeth Brown, Calvert County school teacher who fought for equal pay regardless of race and became the catalyst for the Maryland Teachers Pay Equalization Law, the first equalization law in Maryland.

highway_name: Dedicated to Delegate Tyras Bunk Athey
source_note: Dedicated to Tyras Snowden “Bunk” Athey, Burtonsville native, former MD Secretary of the State who served in the MD House of Delegates from 1967 to 1993.

highway_name: Dedicated to Dr Charles I Ecker
source_note: Dedicated to Charles Isaac Ecker, Maryland native, a career public school administrator and County Executive of Howard County from 1990 to 1998.

highway_name: Dedicated to Paul Kerman
source_note: Dedicated to Paul Kerman who developed Drum Point Beach and served on the Maryland state judicial selection commission.

highway_name: This Highway Dedicated to Senator Charles H. Smelser
source_note: Dedicated to Charles H. “Bus” Smelser, Uniontown native who represented Carroll and Frederick counties in the MD House of Delegates from 1955 to 1963 and the MD Senate from 1967 to 1994.

highway_name: Dedicated to OFC. William D. ""Bill"" Talbert  Montgomery County Police Department
source_note: Dedicated to Montgomery County Police Officer William D. “Bill” Talbert who was severely injured in the line of duty when struck by a drunk driver and ultimately perished from related complications several years later.

highway_name: Dedicated to Governor Robert L. Ehrlich, Jr.
source_note: Dedicated to Robert L. Ehrlich, Jr., 60th Governor of Maryland, who prioritized construction of the Intercounty Connector (MD-200) during his administration. This all-electronic toll road opened in 2011 and planning efforts date back to the 1950’s.

highway_name: Dedicated to Capt. Joseph Kroboth, Jr. Halfway Volunteer Fire Co
source_note: Dedicated to Volunteer Fire Company of Halfway Captain Joseph Kroboth, Jr. who was killed in the line of duty on May 3, 1998 while directing traffic at the scene of a motor vehicle collision.

highway_name: Dedicated to Senator Laurence Levitan Maryland State Senate
source_note: Dedicated to Laurence Levitan who represented District 15 in both the MD House of and MD Senate for over two decades. Senator Levitan was also the Chair of the Budget and Taxation Committee from 1979 to 1994.

highway_name: Dedicated to Del. K. Bennett Bozman Maryland House of Delegates
source_note: Dedicated to K. Bennett Bozman who advocated for the dualization of US-113 and represented Wicomico and Worcester Counties in the MD House of Delegates from 1991 until his death in 2006.

highway_name: Dedicated to Sgt. Anthony M. Walker Prince George's County Police Dept
source_note: Dedicated to Prince George’s County Police Sergeant Anthony “Tony” Michael Walker, President of Prince George’s County Fraternal Order of Police, who succumbed to injuries sustained while on patrol in an automobile accident on November 15, 2003.

highway_name: Dedicated to Sergeant Richard S. Findley Prince George's County Police Dept.
source_note: Dedicated to Prince George’s County Police Sergeant Richard Scott Findley, who was killed in the line of duty when he was intentionally struck by the driver of a stolen automobile on June 27, 2008.

highway_name: Dedicated to Thomas M. Yeager
source_note: Dedicated to Thomas Merle Yeager, who was a member of the Howard County Council from 1975 to 1983 and represented newly formed District 13 in the MD Senate from 1983 to 1994.

highway_name: Dedicated to Delegate Norman H Conway
source_note: Dedicated to Norman H. Conway, Salisbury native and former President of Salisbury City Council (1982-1987), who represented District 38B in the MD House of Delegates from 1987 to 2015.

highway_name: Dedicated to SSGT Charles I. Cartwright Untied States Marine Corps
source_note: Dedicated to United States Marine Corps Staff Sergeant Charles I. Cartwright who was killed in action in Farah province, Afghanistan on November 7, 2009 while supporting Operation Enduring Freedom.

highway_name: Key Highway
source_note: Named in honor of Francis Scott Key, Maryland native and author of our national anthem, "The Star-Spangled Banner."

highway_name: Stephanie Roper Highway
source_note: Dedicated to Stephanie Roper, whose 1982 murder was the catalyst for the creation of a number of victim’s assistance services. It prompted her parents,  Roberta and Vincent Roper, to form the Maryland Crime Victims’ Resource Center, formerly known as the Stephanie Roper Committee and Foundation.

highway_name: This Interchange Dedicated to Bishop James R. Peebles Sr.
source_note: Dedicated to Bishop James R. Peebles, Sr., who formed nearby Jericho City of Praise with his wife in 1969.

highway_name: Dedicated to DFC James Yvorra
source_note: Dedicated to Prince George's Fire Department Deputy Fire Chief James G. Yvorra who was killed in the line of duty when he was struck by an automobile while on the scene of an accident on January 28, 1988.

highway_name: Dedicated to Maryland Natural Resources Police
source_note: Dedicated to the Maryland Natural Resources Police, Maryland’s oldest state police force and one of the nation’s oldest conservation law enforcement agencies, which celebrated 150 years of service in 2018.

highway_name: Dedicated to Senator Norman Stone
source_note: Dedicated to Senator Norman R. Stone, Jr., Baltimore native, who represented District 6 in the in the Maryland State Senate for 12 terms from 1967 to 2015. Senator Stone is the longest serving senator in Maryland history.

highway_name: Dedicated to Donnie L Drewer
source_note: Dedicated to Donnie L. Drewer, Crisfield native, who gave 55 years of dedicated service to the MDOT State Highway Administration and directly influenced nearly every major roadway project in Dorchester, Somerset, Wicomico and Worcester counties from 1988 to 2016.

highway_name: Dedicated to Delegate Theodore J Sophocleus
source_note: Dedicated to Theodore J. Sophocleus, prominent area political official and longtime public servant who represented District 32 in the Maryland House of Delegates from 1999 to 2018.

highway_name: Dedicated to LCPL Matthew A Snyder
source_note: Dedicated to United States Marine Corps Lance Corporal Matthew A. Snyder, Finksburg native and Westminster High School alumnus, who was killed in action in the Anbar Province, Iraq on March 3, 2006 while supporting Operation Iraqi Freedom.

highway_name: Dedicated to Champions of Voter Reform
source_note: Dedicated to the ten men of the North Dorchester Democratic Club who in 1986 successfully fought to change the statewide county council election process from at-large voting to a district voting system. The nearby Historical Freedom Shrine commemorates the amendment to the Maryland State Constitution and declares North Dorchester County as the “Birthplace of Voter Reform.”

highway_name: SGT Sherwood Reynolds US Army Vietnam War Killed in Action 2-26-68
source_note: Dedicated to United States Army Sergeant Sherwood Reynolds, Prince Frederick native, who was killed in action on February 26, 1968 while supporting the Vietnam War. Sergeant Reynolds was the first service member from Calvert County to perish in combat in Vietnam.

highway_name: Dedicated to Officer Oliver Smith, Jr
source_note: Dedicated to Metropolitan Police Officer Oliver Wendell Smith, Jr. who was killed in the line of duty during a robbery while off duty on February 26, 1997.

highway_name: Dedicated to Senator James Ed DeGrange, Sr.
source_note: Dedicated to Senator James “Ed” DeGrange, Sr., prominent area political official who represented District 32 in the Maryland State Senate from 1999 to 2018.

highway_name: Dedicated to PFC Russell Milberry United State Army
source_note: Dedicated to United States Army Private First Class Russell E. Milberry, Union Bridge native, who was killed in action in the Quang Tri province of Vietnam on January 31, 1968 while supporting the Vietnam War.

highway_name: Dedicated to PS1 Xavier A Martin United States Navy
source_note: Dedicated to United States Navy Petty Officer First Class Xavier A. Martin, Halethorpe native, who perished along with 6 others on the U.S.S. Fitzgerald on June 17, 2017.

highway_name: Dedicated to Brianna N Freeman
source_note: In a car crash that took the life of 10 month old Brianna Nicole Freeman, Brianna gave the gift of life to 3 others; her liver to a 2 month old, her kidneys to a 57 year old & her heart to a 3 year old.

highway_name: Dedicated to Chief Daniel Lister Queen Anne - Hillsboro VFC
source_note: The assistant chief of the Queen Anne-Hillsboro Volunteer Fire Company died while working on a scene of a head-on crash with several people entrapped in the wreckage (Aug 2018).

highway_name: Dedicated to Sheriff Joseph Lee Somerville St. Mary's County
source_note: In 1977, Joseph Lee Somerville became both the first black sheriff in St. Mary’s County & in the state of Maryland when he was appointed by the governor to complete the previous sheriff’s term. Somerville went on to win the 1978 election for St. Mary’s County Sheriff, which then made him the first elected Black sheriff in St. Mary’s County & in Maryland as well.

highway_name: Dedicated to SPC Toccara Green United States Army
source_note: SPC Toccara Green died in Al Asad, Iraq, when multiple improvised explosive devices detonated near her unit during convoy operations. She was assigned to the 57th Transportation Company, 548th Corps Support Battalion, Fort Drum, New York. Died on August 14, 2005.

highway_name: Designated as Piscataway Highway
Dedicated to Hon Henry B ""Hank"" Heller
source_note: Henry B. “Hank” Heller, was a Democrat who represented Montgomery County in the House of Delegates for two dozen years. Heller, a longtime educator & union activist who served as president of the Montgomery County Education Association, was elected to represent District 19, which takes in portions of Silver Spring and unincorporated Rockville, in 1986.

highway_name: Dedicated to Senator George C Edwards
source_note: Senator George Edwards was elected as the State Senator for Maryland District 1 in 2006, which covers Garrett County and parts of Washington and Allegany counties. He defeated Thomas Conlon, both of whom ran for the seat vacated by John J. Hafer who retired after 16 years in the State Senate. Edwards was first elected to the Maryland House of Delegates in 1982 and served for 24 years before running and winning the State Senate seat.

highway_name: Dedicated to SGT Mujahid Ramzziddin
source_note: Sergeant Mujahid Ramzziddin was shot and killed at approximately 10:15 am while attempting to protect a female subject who was being attacked in a domestic violence incident. Sergeant Ramzziddin, who was off duty and lived nearby, attempted to protect the female and confronted the man who was armed with a shotgun.

highway_name: Dedicated to Senator Ed Reilly
source_note: The nine-mile portion of Maryland Route 450 from Crofton to Annapolis known as Defense Highway will be officially renamed after Reilly on October 25, 2023. Reilly worked on solutions to flooding along the road in 2018 & 2019.

highway_name: Dedicated to Deputy Chief Sander B Cohen Office of the State Fire Marshall
source_note: Deputy Chief Sander B. Cohen was killed on December 8, 2017 at approximately 10:00 p.m. after stopping to assist FBI Supervisory Special Agent Carlos Wolff who had just been involved in single vehicle accident in the southbound lane of I-270 in the Rockville area.

highway_name: Dedicated to the Honorable Karen Montgomery
source_note: Karen Slater Montgomery (born August 23, 1935) is an American politician and artist from the state of Maryland. She served as the state senator for the Maryland State Senate from Maryland's 14th District, which includes parts of Silver Spring, Burtonsville, Olney, Barnesville, Brookeville, Laytonsville and Damascus in Montgomery County.

highway_name: Dedicated to Commissioner Clyde W Unglesbee
source_note: Mr. Unglesbee was a Town of Brookeville commissioner, charter president and life member of Olney Chamber of Commerce, member of the board of directors of: Montgomery General Hospital; Sandy Spring National Bank; Asbury Methodist Village; and Sandy Spring Museum, serving two years as chairman.

highway_name: Dedicated to PFC Michael A Carter Jr United States Marine Corps
source_note: An 18-year-old Marine, who was back near his Maryland hometown recruiting for the Corps, died in a single-vehicle crash.  PFC. Michael Anthony Carter Jr. died Feb. 7 when his Toyota Camry hit a guardrail and went into a ditch, according to Delmarva Now.

highway_name: Michael R Powers Libertytown VFD 6-25-2019
source_note: Mr. Michael R. Powers, 70, of Libertytown, passed away on Tuesday, June 25, 2019, in the line of duty while operating at an emergency incident scene.

highway_name: Dedicated to Detective Jacai Colson Prince George's Co. Police Dept.
source_note: Corporal Jacai Colson was shot and killed while responding to an active shooter at the Prince George's County Police Department's District III police station at 7600 Barlowe Road on March 13, 2016.

highway_name: Dedicated to PFC Donald H Selby Korean War MIA-POW
source_note: On the night of Feb. 11, 1951 Company K of the U.S. Army’s 3rd Battalion, 9th Regiment, 2nd Infantry Division was operating a task force alongside the Republic of Korea’s 16th Infantry Regiment to push back Chinese Communists during the Korean War. The incursion was brief, but deadly, according to the Defense POW/MIA Accounting Agency, and Private First Class Donald Harold Selby of Oriole was among those captured by the Chinese. After hostilities ended, repatriated POWs reported Pfc Selby died.

highway_name: Dedicated to PFC Thurman B Ellis Army Rangers 1st Batt
source_note: Thurman B Ellis from Jarrettsville, MD served with distinction in WWII. A Private First Class in the US Army he was inducted into the service in February 1943 & received his basic training at Camp McCain, MS. He was assigned to the US Army Rangers Company C 1st Ranger Battalion which was part of a composite force of the 6615th Ranger Force (Provisional) with the 1st, 3rd, and 4th Ranger Battalions, 83rd Chemical Mortar Battalion, & the 509th Parachute Infantry Battalion.
